title: Microscopic evidence for a Zhang-Rice triplet state in the van der Waals antiferromagnet, NiPS$_3$
authors: Beom Hyun Kim, Youjin Lee, Junik Hwang, Junghyun Kim, Je-Geun Park, Seung-Ho Baek
abstract: Quantum-entangled states underpin many emergent phenomena in quantum materials, yet their direct experimental identification remains a challenge. NiPS$_3$, a van der Waals antiferromagnet exhibiting a resolution-limited magnetic exciton in its ordered phase, has been proposed to host a many-body entangled Zhang-Rice triplet state. Here, using $^{33}$S nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) on $^{33}$S-enriched NiPS$_3$ single crystals, we provide microscopic evidence for this charge-transfer state. The $^{33}$S and $^{31}$P Knight shifts as a function of temperature reveal a unified spin-triplet configuration arising from strong hybridization between a self-doped hole in the S $3p$ orbitals and a hole in Ni $3d$ orbitals. Furthermore, the $^{33}$S nuclear spin-lattice relaxation rate exhibits a power-law divergence as it approaches the Néel temperature $T_N=155$ K, indicating critical slowing down of collective charge fluctuations consistent with spin-nematic correlations. These results reveal a spin-charge-intertwined ground state and establish the microscopic foundation for the exceptional coherence of the magnetic exciton in NiPS$_3$.
summary: This experimental study uses $^{33}$S NMR on NiPS3 to probe its electronic structure, arguing that the material hosts a spin-charge-intertwined Zhang-Rice triplet state. By analyzing Knight shifts and observing a power-law divergence in the relaxation rate near the Néel temperature, the authors provide evidence for nematic charge fluctuations driving the magnetic ordering.
main_problem: The study aims to provide microscopic evidence for a Zhang-Rice triplet state in the van der Waals antiferromagnet NiPS3 and understand the origin of its magnetic exciton coherence.
main_result: NMR measurements reveal strong hybridization between S 3p and Ni 3d orbitals, supporting a spin-charge-intertwined ground state. Furthermore, critical slowing down observed via $^{33}$S relaxation suggests nematic correlations drive the magnetic transition.
method: The research employed solid-state Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy on enriched single crystals of NiPS3, measuring Knight shifts and nuclear spin-lattice relaxation rates ($T_1^{-1}$).
model_or_system: The physical system is NiPS3, a van der Waals antiferromagnet. The theoretical framework involves modeling the ground state as a Zhang-Rice triplet (ZRT) state arising from strong hybridization between ligand holes (S 3p) and transition metal moments (Ni 3d).
key_observables: Temperature dependence of $^{33}$S and $^{31}$P Knight shifts, power-law divergence of the $^{33}$S nuclear spin-lattice relaxation rate ($T_1^{-1}$), and the local Electric Field Gradient (EFG) tensor components.
important_parameters:
Néel temperature $T_N = 155$ K; Quadrupole frequency $
u_Q = 6.4 	ext{ MHz}$; Critical exponent related to power-law divergence ($\propto |T - T_0|^{-0.464}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed NMR signals are sensitive probes of local spin and charge fluctuations, allowing for the differentiation between different types of magnetic correlations (e.g., nematic vs. simple AFM).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the crystal structure details related to the EFG tensor components and show the temperature dependence of various spectroscopic measurements, such as Knight shifts and relaxation rates.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the material's magnetic properties (AFM ordering), then using NMR spectroscopy on $^{33}$S and $^{31}$P to probe local spin susceptibility via Knight shifts. Finally, it analyzes the temperature dependence of $T_1^{-1}$ to pinpoint critical charge fluctuations associated with nematic correlations.
why_interesting: The work connects advanced spectroscopic techniques (NMR) directly to fundamental concepts in strongly correlated materials theory, specifically identifying signatures of emergent quantum states like the Zhang-Rice triplet and coupling them to magnetic phase transitions via critical dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The overall spectral shape remains unchanged across the temperature range, with two well-resolved peaks (labelled A and B) persisting up to room temperature. (b) 33S Knight shift 33K of the A and B peaks as a function of temperature. The temperature dependence of both 33K and 31P Knight shift 31K (right axis) closely follows that of the bulk spin susceptibility, indicating that both nuclei probe the identical local spin susceptibility. Note that the two lines for 31P arise from nuclear dipole-dipole interactions, so called Pake doublet [20]. (c) Knight shift versus susceptibility (K-χ) plots with...

(b) Temperature dependence of the reduced relaxation rate, R1 ≡(T1χT )−1. While the nearly constant 31R1 supports the absence of collective spin ﬂuctuations, 33R1 exhibits a power-law divergence, which is well-ﬁtted by Eq. (1) throughout the entire temperature range (solid line). This divergence demonstrates the development of collective charge ﬂuctuations towards an incipient nematic ordering instability (see text).
